mapping.a_element: high-dimensional stochastic state (many parallel heterogeneous queues, empirical queue-length measure)
mapping.b_element: high-dimensional stochastic learning state (Q-values of the interacting learners)

mapping.a_element: limiting deterministic descriptors extracted from the process (limiting fairness process / routing measure via the diffusion limit)
mapping.b_element: expected dynamics of the Q-learning process used to summarize the mean drift

mapping.a_element: asymptotic characterization identifying the optimal tie-breaking regime
mapping.b_element: boundary predicting when time-averaged cooperative dominance arises

brief_justification: The overlap is only the generic fact that both analyze a high-dimensional stochastic process through a reduced deterministic descriptor. The actual machinery differs: paper A builds a heavy-traffic Halfin-Whitt DIFFUSION limit of measure-valued queueing processes and proves asymptotic optimality via coupling-based sample-path comparisons, whereas paper B studies the time-averaged stationary behavior of an epsilon-greedy Q-learning Markov chain and derives a stability boundary from its expected (drift) dynamics in a repeated game. No role-for-role correspondence holds between a queueing diffusion approximation and a learning-dynamics stability boundary.
